description: Create a three-column-plus text content slide on Technology Vision 2025 AI autonomy and trust, with an introduction header and long-form narrative text blocks about trust foundations, systems, AI, and people.

left.body:
Half of workers using AI are reluctant to admit it and worry that using it for important tasks makes them look replaceable. This reflects how AI is shaking up the trusted relationship between people and their employer. Employees are used to well-developed career paths, defined roles, skill expectations, and a shared understanding of how work performance translates into job stability, and AI is bringing uncertainty to this.

For enterprises, trust is a crucial currency underpinning relationships with customers, employees, regulators, and shareholders. Until now, trust was built in small moments, many of which AI is changing. Everyday micro-interactions across businesses—from sales and support to service, identity confirmation, and delivery—can and will be disrupted by AI. Many may improve through more autonomy, less friction, and better outcomes, but leaders must ask how far they can go before trust becomes an issue and how to reinvigorate the critical human moments that build it.

These are the issues leaders need to tackle. Autonomy is key to the next generation of business growth and innovation. Employees should be able to work more autonomously with fleets of agents at their command, and customers should be able to interact freely with autonomous enterprise systems with on-demand purchasing, customization, and relevance at scale. But that autonomy must be facilitated by trust across customers, leaders, systems, workers, and AI.

This is why trust is not simply one of many trends in this year’s report. It is the consideration. As every company begins reinventing itself with generalized AI, technology alone cannot be the sole focus. Reaping AI’s benefits will only be possible when built on a foundation of trust, making this every leader’s first priority.

center.title: Pathways to a Stronger Foundation
center.body:
Trust is not gone in the world of AI, but it is becoming far more dynamic and essential to enterprise plans. Leaders will need to navigate both the emotional and cognitive dimensions of trust. Emotional dimensions include whether people fear AI, feel aligned with it, or feel taken advantage of by it, and these concerns will require real policy and governance as enterprises diffuse the technology more broadly.

These efforts must be complemented by action on the cognitive dimensions of trust: whether a system acts reliably and competently, can navigate challenges, and still behaves as expected within the guardrails laid out for it. This is especially important for autonomous AI systems that are relied on to learn, grow, and act based on intent rather than explicit direction.

Supporting this will require dedicated teams of domain and decision scientists, or AI Ops teams, who constantly test, evaluate, and improve the accuracy, predictability, consistency, and explainability needed to maintain cognitive trust in systems. There is no one-size-fits-all solution. Every enterprise has different trust-building moments, technologies, AI strategies, and key relationships, but broadly the path forward centers on addressing trust across systems and data, AI itself, and people.

right.body:
First, enterprises need to bolster the cybersecurity and trust of their digital systems. Building this new foundation does not mean starting from scratch; many prior technology and strategy investments can pay new dividends. Cybersecurity strategies such as zero trust and entity behavior analytics will be critical. With AI’s dependence on data, protecting systems, people, and data is increasingly important. Distributed ledger technologies can also foster ecosystem-scale trust networks by ensuring entities comply with agreed rules. Strong cybersecurity overall will be paramount to achieving AI trust and security.

The second dimension of the roadmap is building trust in AI itself. Responsible AI is becoming an established discipline that enterprises will increasingly rely on to ethically steward their strategies. Techniques such as explainability, transparency around data collection, debiasing, and related practices will become a critical bridge between technical solutioning and the humans interested in using the technology. Questions about how AI is trained, who it works for, and how it makes decisions are unavoidable, so enterprises need to be prepared with answers now.

Lastly, the third and uncharted part of the roadmap is finding a new path to people-driven trust. Organizations need new touchpoints and ways to establish and maintain trust as AI disrupts traditional interactions. The starting point is self-directed questions: what career paths look like when many entry-level jobs can be done by AI, what establishes job security for employees who use AI to streamline their work, and how to maintain a personal touch with customers if frontline support is AI agents. Enterprises should answer these in ways that promote the potential of the symbiotic relationship between people and AI.
